This family includes over 150 genera and more than 3,000 species, many of which are native to Australia and the Pacific islands. Myrtus chequen specifically belongs to the genus Myrtus, which consists of a small number of species, primarily native to the Mediterranean region and the Pacific islands. The genus Myrtus is further divided into several species, each with distinct characteristics and distributions. Overall, the classification of Myrtus chequen reflects its unique position within the larger family of Myrtaceae and the genus Myrtus.

It is believed to have anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antioxidant properties, which contribute to its medicinal uses. Traditionally, the leaves and branches of Myrtus chequen are used to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and coughs.
